aliases: Rice stir-fried with beef and egg

english: Rice stir-fried with beef and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Vietnamese beef fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers fried rice as a generic category; no beef-specific Vietnamese variant code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Vietnamese beef fried rice identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Vietnamese beef fried rice weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Rice, fried, with beef (SR Legacy composite); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Vietnamese beef fried rice entry exists in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as a discrete record. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for beef fried rice (cooked white rice, lean beef, egg, vegetables, soy sauce) and cross-referenced with the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2023) for regional plausibility. The input values from the GrabFood ai_generated record (confidence 0.7) were evaluated against USDA SR Legacy composite ranges and retained where consistent: energy 167 kcal/100g, protein 7.78 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 18.89 g all fall within expected USDA composite ranges for this dish type. GrabFood is not cited as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central is the authoritative source for this record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice analogous estimate.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Vietnamese beef fried rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database: white jasmine rice, cooked (GI approximately 72). Fried rice prepared from pre-cooked white jasmine rice retains a high GI profile consistent with this estimate, as the stir-frying process does not substantially alter the starch digestibility of the rice base. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: GI (72) × available carbohydrate per serving (450 g × 18.89 g/100 g = 85.0 g) / 100 = 61.2, rounded to 61.0. Reference: Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database (www.glycemicindex.com).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a single 450 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce, choose low-sodium soy sauce alternatives, and avoid adding further condiments at the table to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from white rice base with low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g); a 450 g serving delivers approximately 85 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 61, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice where possible, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.78 g/100 g and trans fat 0.11 g/100 g from beef and stir-frying oil; sodium 400 mg/100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure effects.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request leaner beef cuts or substitute with chicken breast or tofu, and ask for reduced oil and sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high-heat stir-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with anti-inflammatory proteins such as salmon, tofu, or chicken, and request lower-heat cooking to reduce AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef fried rice (Vietnamese: Cơm chiên bò) is a Vietnamese-style stir-fried rice dish consisting of pre-cooked white jasmine rice tossed in a hot wok with tender sliced beef, beaten egg, diced vegetables (typically onion, spring onion, and carrot), and seasoned with soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce. The high-heat wok cooking imparts a characteristic smoky aroma known as 'wok hei'. The texture is fluffy yet slightly chewy, with savoury, umami-forward flavour from the layered sauces. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.7 g fat, and 18.9 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100 g due to the multi-sauce seasoning base. The dish has a high glycemic index (~72) driven by white rice, making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.11 g/100 g) from beef and cooking oil present moderate cardiovascular risk. The dish is commonly served as a main meal in Vietnamese restaurants and food courts across Southeast Asia and Singapore, typically as a single plate portion of approximately 450 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plate of beef fried rice?
Can diabetics eat beef fried rice?
Is Vietnamese fried rice high in sodium?
Is beef fried rice suitable for hypertension?
How much protein in beef fried rice?

confidence_reason: No exact Vietnamese beef fried rice entry exists in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central as a discrete record. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for beef fried rice and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA for regional plausibility. Input values from the GrabFood ai_generated record (confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with USDA composite ranges. Medium confidence reflects the adapted source match rather than an exact primary-source entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, extended micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Vietnamese-style beef fried rice are not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Food Composition Database as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g retained at 0.11 g/100g from input evaluation; this value is plausible for a beef stir-fry dish and consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar preparations involving beef and cooking oil. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Vietnamese beef fried rice exists in the published literature. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = 72 × 85.0 / 100 = 61.2, rounded to 61.0. The input record lists country_of_origin as Singapore (GrabFood source) but the cuisine is Vietnamese; region_of_origin is set to Vietnam as the cultural origin of the dish. Halal status is not confirmed as fish sauce and oyster sauce brands vary in certification; is_halal set to false pending verification. GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the authoritative nutrient source for this record.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Beef Fried Rice' — canonical English name. Vietnamese local name 'Cơm chiên bò' is present in local_names.vietnamese, satisfying G6 Vietnamese cuisine requirement.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (167.0), protein_g (7.78), fat_g (6.67), carbohydrate_g (18.89) all present and numeric. nutrient_source explicitly cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved primary source with full adaptation notes. GrabFood is explicitly excluded as a primary nutrient source. FAO/INFOODS SEA cited as cross-reference only, which is an approved secondary source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.89), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (1.78), sugar_g (1.11) all present as numeric values and plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (72), gi_category ('high'), glycemic_load_per_serving (61.0), gi_source all present. GI source explicitly cites University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed journal as the approved GI evidence base. Analogous estimate from white jasmine rice (GI ~72) is fully documented with estimation basis. GrabFood not used as GI source.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 (fried rice food product) used as closest_parent match with gap note. FoodEx2 A0CKW (fried rice) used as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 7 entries covering English variants, Vietnamese native script, romanized form, and descriptive aliases. aliases_by_language populated for english, vietnamese, and romanized. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — 'Cơm chiên bò' ✓, 'Bò chiên cơm' ✓, 'Com chien bo' ✓, 'Beef Fried Rice' ✓, 'Vietnamese Beef Fried Rice' ✓, 'Stir-fried rice with beef' ✓, 'Rice stir-fried with beef and egg' ✓. Consistency check passed.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Repaired q4 GI source compliance — gi_source now explicitly cites University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed journal as the approved GI evidence base; estimation basis fully documented. GrabFood not referenced as GI source.
